children.name: Cardinalis
children.description: The true cardinals — crested, thick-billed passerines named for the brilliant red plumage of the male, which resembles the robes of Catholic cardinals. They are among the most recognisable and popular birds in the Americas, with a rich vocabulary of whistled songs. Unlike many songbirds, both sexes of the Northern Cardinal sing, often duetting with their mate.

children.name: Cardinalis cardinalis
children.description: One of the most beloved and recognisable birds in North America, with the male a brilliant crimson red with a black face mask and prominent crest. The female is a warm buff-brown with red tinges on the crest, wings, and tail. It was once a southern bird but has expanded dramatically northward into Canada, aided by backyard bird feeders. Its rich whistled song and year-round presence make it a favourite at winter feeding stations.

children.name: Cardinalis sinuatus
children.description: The desert cardinal of the southwestern United States and Mexico, distinguished from the Northern Cardinal by its greyish body, red face mask, crest, and breast, and a thick, curved yellow bill that resembles a parrot's. It inhabits arid scrublands, mesquite thickets, and desert washes. Its name combines 'pyrrhos' (flame-coloured) and 'loxos' (crosswise), referring to its crossed-looking bill.

children.name: Pheucticus
children.description: The New World grosbeaks — large, heavy-billed songbirds with massive conical bills adapted for cracking hard seeds and nuts. Males are boldly patterned in black, white, and bright yellow or rose-red; females are more subdued brown and buff. They are primarily forest and woodland birds, foraging in the canopy and often visiting feeders for sunflower seeds.

children.name: Pheucticus ludovicianus
children.description: A striking songbird of eastern North American forests, the male is black and white with a vivid triangular rose-red patch on the breast. Its song is a rich, robin-like warble that it delivers while feeding and moving through the canopy. It winters from Mexico through Central America to the northern Andes. The female incubates eggs while the male sings from a nearby perch in a shared nesting territory.

children.name: Pheucticus melanocephalus
children.description: The western counterpart of the Rose-breasted Grosbeak, with a black head, orange-breasted body, and black-and-white wings. It inhabits riparian woodlands, oak forests, and suburban gardens across western North America. Its song is similar to that of a robin but richer and more melodious, delivered with a burry, operatic quality. It is one of the few birds that can safely eat monarch butterflies.

children.name: Passerina
children.description: The New World buntings — small, brilliantly coloured seed-eating birds that rank among the most beautiful of all North American songbirds. Males in breeding plumage display vivid blues, indigos, lazulis, and greens; females are soft browns and buffs. They build compact cup nests in shrubs and sing sweet, complex, hurried songs from exposed perches. Young males take two years to attain full adult plumage.

children.name: Passerina caerulea
children.description: A large, heavy-billed bunting that resembles an oversized Indigo Bunting, with the male a deep rich blue with two rusty wing bars. It favours shrubby fields, overgrown pastures, and roadside thickets across the southern and central United States. Its song is a rich, warbled series of notes, and it feeds its young an unusually high proportion of grasshoppers and crickets.

children.name: Passerina cyanea
children.description: A small, vivid blue finch-like bird that appears to glow electric blue in direct sunlight — though the colour is structural, not pigment-based, and vanishes if the feathers are backlit. It breeds in brushy fields and woodland edges across eastern North America and migrates to Mexico and Central America. It navigates at night using the stars, orienting by the night sky during its nocturnal migrations.

children.name: Passerina amoena
children.description: The western counterpart of the Indigo Bunting, the male is a brilliant turquoise-blue above with a rusty breast, white belly, and white wing bars. It inhabits brushy hillsides, chaparral, and riparian thickets in western North America. Its song is a hurried, jumbled warble, and it frequently hybridises with the Indigo Bunting where the two species meet in the Great Plains.

children.name: Passerina ciris
children.description: Arguably the most brilliantly coloured bird in North America, the male combines a blue head, green back, red underparts, and red rump in a pattern that seems painted by an artist. It breeds in dense thickets and scrub along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts and in the south-central United States. The female is a uniform bright green, providing excellent camouflage while incubating.

children.name: Spiza
children.description: The Dickcissel — a monotypic genus of grassland bird that resembles a small meadowlark but is actually a member of the cardinal family. It is named for its distinctive song, a dry, buzzing 'dick-dick-cissel' delivered from an exposed fence post or weed stalk. It is locally common but notably irruptive, with populations fluctuating dramatically from year to year.

children.name: Spiza americana
children.description: A sparrow-sized bird of tallgrass prairies and hayfields, the male has a yellow breast with a black V, a yellow face, and a dark crown. Its song is a dry, insect-like 'dick-dick-cissel' that carries across open fields. It breeds colonially in the central United States and winters in vast flocks on the llanos of Venezuela and Colombia. It has declined with the loss of native prairie habitat.
